fullTitle: NIST Cloud Computing Security Publications — SP 800-144 and SP 800-210
publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), United States
verificationNote: Content paraphrased from NIST SP 800-144 (2011) and SP 800-210 (2020). Both are publicly available at nvlpubs.nist.gov. Section references provided for source traceability.

publications.fullTitle: Guidelines on Security and Privacy in Public Cloud Computing
publications.scope: Provides guidance on the security and privacy challenges of public cloud computing, targeting organisations planning to outsource data, applications, or infrastructure to public cloud environments. While published in 2011, the foundational security principles remain relevant and are referenced by later NIST publications.

recommendations: Establish a comprehensive cloud governance framework before adoption, defining roles, responsibilities, and risk tolerance for cloud usage
Extend existing information security and privacy programmes to address cloud-specific risks including multi-tenancy, jurisdictional issues, and shared responsibility
Ensure organisational security policies explicitly address cloud computing scenarios and are updated as cloud usage evolves
Assess regulatory implications of cloud deployment, particularly data residency, cross-border data transfer, and sector-specific compliance obligations
Ensure cloud service agreements include audit rights, breach notification obligations, and data ownership clauses
Evaluate the legal and regulatory environment of the jurisdiction where cloud data will be stored and processed
Clearly define the division of security responsibilities between cloud provider and consumer for each service model (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS)
Verify cloud provider security practices through independent audits, certifications, and transparency reports rather than relying solely on contractual claims
Assess the security of the cloud supply chain including sub-processors, datacenter operators, and third-party service components
Plan cloud architecture to minimise attack surface, including network segmentation, least-privilege access, and defence-in-depth controls
Implement robust identity and access management with federation support, multi-factor authentication, and centralised credential management
Design for portability and interoperability to avoid vendor lock-in and enable cloud exit strategies
Encrypt data at rest and in transit, maintaining control over encryption keys where feasible
Implement data classification to apply appropriate protection levels based on sensitivity and regulatory requirements
Establish clear data retention and secure disposal procedures, including verification that data is irrecoverably destroyed when no longer needed
Develop cloud-specific incident response plans addressing shared responsibility for detection, investigation, and recovery
Ensure the ability to collect and preserve forensic evidence in cloud environments, accounting for ephemeral resources and shared infrastructure
Establish clear communication channels and escalation procedures with cloud providers for security incidents

identifiedChallenges: Multi-tenancy risks — resource sharing between tenants creates potential for side-channel attacks and data leakage
Loss of physical control — data and processing are hosted on infrastructure not owned or operated by the organisation
Dependency on provider — service availability, security posture, and business continuity depend on the cloud provider's practices
Data residency — data may be stored or processed in jurisdictions with different privacy and data protection laws
Incident response complexity — shared responsibility creates ambiguity in detection, investigation, and remediation
Insider threat — cloud provider staff have privileged access to customer infrastructure and data

publications.fullTitle: General Access Control Guidance for Cloud Systems
publications.scope: Provides detailed access control guidance for cloud service models (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS), addressing the unique access control challenges introduced by cloud computing including multi-tenancy isolation, abstraction layers, virtualisation, and provider-consumer responsibility boundaries.

recommendations: Implement virtual network isolation using cloud-native constructs (VPCs, VNets, security groups) with deny-by-default policies
Use cloud-native identity services for workload authentication rather than embedding credentials in instances or images
Apply security group rules at both the subnet and instance level for defence-in-depth network access control
Implement separate administrative access paths for infrastructure management and application access
Control hypervisor and physical access exclusively through cloud provider SLAs, audits, and certifications
Leverage platform-provided identity and access management services for application authentication and authorisation
Implement API-level access controls with authentication, rate limiting, and input validation on all platform service interfaces
Use managed identity or workload identity features to eliminate credential management for platform service interactions
Apply the principle of least privilege to platform service permissions, scoping access to specific resources and actions
Ensure development and staging environments have separate access control policies from production
Integrate SaaS applications with enterprise identity providers through SAML or OIDC federation for centralised authentication
Configure SaaS application-level access controls (sharing, collaboration, data export) to align with organisational data classification policies
Monitor and audit SaaS user activities through available audit logs and CASB integration
Implement conditional access policies restricting SaaS access based on device compliance, network location, and risk level
Regularly review and certify SaaS application permissions, especially OAuth consent grants and API scopes
Implement centralised identity governance across all cloud service models using a single identity provider with consistent policies
Apply the principle of least privilege across all service models, regularly reviewing and right-sizing permissions
Implement just-in-time and just-enough-access for privileged operations across cloud environments
Use cloud access security brokers (CASBs) for visibility and policy enforcement across multiple cloud services
Establish break-glass procedures for emergency access with appropriate logging and post-incident review

accessControlPatterns.description: Access decisions based on attributes of the subject, resource, environment, and action. Highly suited to cloud environments where resources are dynamic and tagged with metadata.
accessControlPatterns.cloudExample: AWS IAM policies using condition keys (aws:RequestedRegion, aws:PrincipalOrgID, ec2:ResourceTag) to constrain access based on resource attributes.

accessControlPatterns.description: Access granted based on predefined roles that bundle permissions. The most common access control model in cloud platforms.
accessControlPatterns.cloudExample: Azure built-in roles (Reader, Contributor, Owner) and custom roles assigned at management group, subscription, resource group, or resource scope.

accessControlPatterns.description: Centralised policies that enforce access control decisions across multiple cloud services and accounts. Enables organisation-wide guardrails.
accessControlPatterns.cloudExample: AWS Service Control Policies (SCPs) applied at the organisational unit level to restrict what actions any account in the OU can perform.

accessControlPatterns.description: Extending on-premises or centralised identity to cloud services through SAML, OIDC, or WS-Federation protocols. Eliminates cloud-native credential sprawl.
accessControlPatterns.cloudExample: SAML federation between on-premises Active Directory and multiple SaaS applications through Azure AD/Entra ID or Okta.

relatedNISTPublications.title: The NIST Definition of Cloud Computing
relatedNISTPublications.relevance: Foundational definitions of cloud service models (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS), deployment models (public, private, hybrid, community), and essential characteristics.

relatedNISTPublications.title: Cloud Computing Synopsis and Recommendations
relatedNISTPublications.relevance: Overview of cloud computing benefits, issues, and suitability assessment guidance for federal agencies.

relatedNISTPublications.title: Security and Privacy Controls for Information Systems and Organizations
relatedNISTPublications.relevance: Comprehensive control catalogue applicable to cloud systems. CCM v4 maps extensively to SP 800-53 controls.

relatedNISTPublications.title: NIST Cloud Computing Standards Roadmap
relatedNISTPublications.relevance: Identifies standards relevant to cloud computing adoption, including security, interoperability, and portability standards.

relatedNISTPublications.title: NIST Cloud Computing Reference Architecture
relatedNISTPublications.relevance: Defines cloud computing actors (provider, consumer, broker, carrier, auditor) and their interactions. Useful for understanding shared responsibility.

malaysiaNexus: NIST cloud security publications provide supplementary architecture and access control guidance for Malaysian organisations subject to BNM RMiT cloud security requirements (para 10.49-10.65) and NACSA Act 854 NCII obligations. While not directly mandated, NIST SP 800-210 access control patterns align with RMiT requirements for logical access controls, privileged access management, and multi-factor authentication in cloud environments. Organisations running NCII workloads in cloud should reference SP 800-210 alongside CSA CCM for comprehensive coverage.
